rationale_for_baseline_tier: A colicky post-gluten flare with continued flatus, no vomiting, no fever and no localised peritonism is most likely functional and can be managed with an antispasmodic and simple measures — a T2 baseline with prompt review. Because the abdomen cannot be palpated remotely and distension raises an obstruction question, the threshold to escalate to same-day in-person assessment (T3) or ED (T4) on red flags is deliberately low.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Complete inability to pass wind or motions with bilious/green vomiting and worsening distension
escalation_edges.rationale: Suggests mechanical bowel obstruction requiring imaging and surgical review.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you completely stop passing wind and motions and start vomiting (especially green fluid) with a more swollen tummy, go to the emergency department.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Constant severe pain, pain localising to the lower right, fever, or pain far worse than the tummy feels to press
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the pain becomes constant and severe, moves to your lower right side, you get a fever, or it feels far worse than your tummy is to touch, go to the emergency department.

contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Keep a low threshold for in-person assessment; do not rely on remote reassurance for a distended abdomen.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This is most likely a gut flare from the large gluten meal. Use your antispasmodic, sip clear fluids, apply gentle heat and move around a little — it should ease over the next several hours. Because I can't examine your tummy over video, if the warning signs below appear, get checked in person.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: GP within 3-5 days; ED tonight if red flags
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: Attend ED overnight if obstruction/peritonism features develop; otherwise this can settle at home.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Telehealth review of an acute post-gluten coeliac/functional flare; obstruction/appendicitis/ischaemia screened negative; antispasmodic advised with a strict surgical-abdomen safety-net; coeliac confirmation/dietitian follow-up recommended.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Reassuring a distended abdomen with no surgical red-flag screen or safety-net.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An early small-bowel obstruction is missed and progresses to strangulation and perforation overnight.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Routine review is acceptable only with a strict same-night surgical safety-net.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Immediate ED/CT for a resolving functional flare with no red flags is over-triage.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Cannot palpate/auscultate the abdomen or image remotely to exclude obstruction.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Manage symptomatically with a strict surgical-abdomen safety-net and a low threshold for in-person assessment.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either default to a midnight CT/ED or reassure a distended abdomen without any red-flag screen.
